title_query: Heterogeneous elasticity drives ripening and controls bursting kinetics of transcriptional condensates

semantic_scholar.query: Heterogeneous elasticity drives ripening and controls bursting kinetics of transcriptional condensates

attempts.query: Heterogeneous elasticity drives ripening and controls bursting kinetics of transcriptional condensates

best_candidate.title: Heterogeneous elasticity drives ripening and controls bursting kinetics of transcriptional condensates
best_candidate.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10962985, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

journal.name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

best_candidate.abstract: Significance Biomolecular condensates often exist in elastic mediums, such as transcriptional condensates; however, traditional theories of phase separation are limited to liquid environments. Here, we study the dynamical behaviors of condensates in a chromatin-like environment modeled as a heterogeneous elastic medium. We demonstrate that the ripening process in such an elastic medium exhibits a temporal power-law scaling of the average condensate radius. Moreover, by introducing an active process to model the dissolution of transcriptional condensates beyond thermal equilibrium, we uncover three distinct kinetics of condensate growth, corresponding to different gene expression temporal patterns, with the distributions of kinetic parameters in agreement with experimental data. Our work demonstrates the intricate interplay between biomolecular condensates and elastic mediums.

journal.name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

data.abstract: Significance Biomolecular condensates often exist in elastic mediums, such as transcriptional condensates; however, traditional theories of phase separation are limited to liquid environments. Here, we study the dynamical behaviors of condensates in a chromatin-like environment modeled as a heterogeneous elastic medium. We demonstrate that the ripening process in such an elastic medium exhibits a temporal power-law scaling of the average condensate radius. Moreover, by introducing an active process to model the dissolution of transcriptional condensates beyond thermal equilibrium, we uncover three distinct kinetics of condensate growth, corresponding to different gene expression temporal patterns, with the distributions of kinetic parameters in agreement with experimental data. Our work demonstrates the intricate interplay between biomolecular condensates and elastic mediums.
